era_context: Sargon II's Neo-Assyrian Empire is consolidating its vast territorial holdings. The Levant, Mesopotamia, and parts of Anatolia are under Assyrian control. In China, the Spring and Autumn Period continues with regional states competing for hegemony. Greece is in the Archaic Period, with the development of the hoplite phalanx and the establishment of colonial settlements.

events.title: Sargon II's administrative reorganization and construction of Dur-Sharrukin
events.description: Sargon II undertakes the construction of Dur-Sharrukin (modern Khorsabad), a new royal capital designed to showcase Assyrian power and administrative sophistication. The city is built with monumental palaces, temples, and administrative buildings. Construction and decoration of the palace complex involve skilled artisans and workers from across the empire. The project reflects Sargon's ambitions to create a new center of imperial power and is part of his broader program of cultural and political consolidation.

sources.name: Sargon II's inscriptions and building records

events.certainty_note: The construction of Dur-Sharrukin is well-attested archaeologically. The specific dating of construction phases is based on stratigraphic evidence and inscriptional records, with uncertainty of ±1–2 years.
cross_references: Dur-Sharrukin remains a major Assyrian center until Sargon's death in 705 BCE

disconfirming_evidence: The exact chronology of Dur-Sharrukin's construction phases is debated among archaeologists. Some scholars argue that construction may have begun earlier or later than traditionally dated. The city's role as a primary capital is also questioned; it may have been one of several important administrative centers rather than the sole capital.
historiographic_note: For 716 BCE, documentation is limited to Assyrian sources and archaeological evidence from Dur-Sharrukin. The perspective of workers and subject peoples is absent. Chinese sources for this year are unreliable. Most of the world has no written records.

graph_edges.note: Dur-Sharrukin remains a major center until Sargon II's death in 705 BCE
